modules.name: django.utils.termcolors
documentation: termcolors.py

functions.name: django.utils.termcolors.colorize
documentation:
Return your text, enclosed in ANSI graphics codes.

Depends on the keyword arguments 'fg' and 'bg', and the contents of
the opts tuple/list.

Return the RESET code if no parameters are given.

Valid colors:
    'black', 'red', 'green', 'yellow', 'blue', 'magenta', 'cyan', 'white'

Valid options:
    'bold'
    'underscore'
    'blink'
    'reverse'
    'conceal'
    'noreset' - string will not be auto-terminated with the RESET code

Examples:
    colorize('hello', fg='red', bg='blue', opts=('blink',))
    colorize()
    colorize('goodbye', opts=('underscore',))
    print(colorize('first line', fg='red', opts=('noreset',)))
    print('this should be red too')
    print(colorize('and so should this'))
    print('this should not be red')

functions.name: django.utils.termcolors.make_style
documentation:
Return a function with default parameters for colorize()

Example:
    bold_red = make_style(opts=('bold',), fg='red')
    print(bold_red('hello'))
    KEYWORD = make_style(fg='yellow')
    COMMENT = make_style(fg='blue', opts=('bold',))

functions.name: django.utils.termcolors.parse_color_setting
documentation:
Parse a DJANGO_COLORS environment variable to produce the system palette

The general form of a palette definition is:

    "palette;role=fg;role=fg/bg;role=fg,option,option;role=fg/bg,option,option"

where:
    palette is a named palette; one of 'light', 'dark', or 'nocolor'.
    role is a named style used by Django
    fg is a foreground color.
    bg is a background color.
    option is a display options.

Specifying a named palette is the same as manually specifying the individual
definitions for each role. Any individual definitions following the palette
definition will augment the base palette definition.

Valid roles:
    'error', 'success', 'warning', 'notice', 'sql_field', 'sql_coltype',
    'sql_keyword', 'sql_table', 'http_info', 'http_success',
    'http_redirect', 'http_not_modified', 'http_bad_request',
    'http_not_found', 'http_server_error', 'migrate_heading',
    'migrate_label'

Valid colors:
    'black', 'red', 'green', 'yellow', 'blue', 'magenta', 'cyan', 'white'

Valid options:
    'bold', 'underscore', 'blink', 'reverse', 'conceal', 'noreset'

modules.name: django.utils.translation.trans_null

Turn any callable into a lazy evaluated callable. result classes or types

functions.name: django.utils.functional.keep_lazy_text

classes.name: django.utils.functional.cached_property

methods.name: django.utils.functional.cached_property.__get__
documentation:
Call the function and put the return value in instance.__dict__ so that
subsequent attribute access on the instance returns the cached value
instead of calling cached_property.__get__().

A cached property can be made out of an existing method:

documentation:
Base class for the proxy class created in the closure of the lazy function.
It's used to recognize promises in code.

classes.name: django.utils.functional.LazyObject

methods.name: django.utils.functional.LazyObject._setup
documentation: Must be implemented by subclasses to initialize the wrapped object.

methods.name: django.utils.functional.LazyObject.__reduce__
documentation: Because we have messed with __class__ below, we confuse pickle as to what
class we are pickling. We're going to have to initialize the wrapped
object to successfully pickle it, so we might as well just pickle the
wrapped object since they're supposed to act the same way.
Unfortunately, if we try to simply act like the wrapped object, the ruse
will break down when pickle gets our id(). Thus we end up with pickle
thinking, in effect, that we are a distinct object from the wrapped
object, but with the same __dict__. This can cause problems (see #25389).
So instead, we define our own __reduce__ method and custom unpickler. We
pickle the wrapped object as the unpickler's argument, so that pickle
will pickle it normally, and then the unpickler simply returns its
argument.

A wrapper for another class that can be used to delay instantiation of the
wrapped class.

By subclassing, you have the opportunity to intercept and alter the
instantiation. If you don't need to do that, use SimpleLazyObject.

classes.name: django.utils.functional.SimpleLazyObject

methods.name: django.utils.functional.SimpleLazyObject.__init__
documentation:
Pass in a callable that returns the object to be wrapped.

If copies are made of the resulting SimpleLazyObject, which can happen
in various circumstances within Django, then you must ensure that the
callable can be safely run more than once and will return the same
value.

methods.name: django.utils.functional.SimpleLazyObject.__repr__
documentation: Return a meaningful representation of the lazy object for debugging
without evaluating the wrapped object.

A lazy object initialized from any function.

Designed for compound objects of unknown type. For builtins or objects of
known type, use django.utils.functional.lazy.

functions.name: django.utils.timesince.timesince
documentation:
Take two datetime objects and return the time between d and now as a nicely
formatted string, e.g. "10 minutes". If d occurs after now, return
"0 minutes".

Units used are years, months, weeks, days, hours, and minutes.
Seconds and microseconds are ignored.

The algorithm takes into account the varying duration of years and months.
There is exactly "1 year, 1 month" between 2013/02/10 and 2014/03/10,
but also between 2007/08/10 and 2008/09/10 despite the delta being 393 days
in the former case and 397 in the latter.

Up to `depth` adjacent units will be displayed.  For example,
"2 weeks, 3 days" and "1 year, 3 months" are possible outputs, but
"2 weeks, 3 hours" and "1 year, 5 days" are not.

`time_strings` is an optional dict of strings to replace the default
TIME_STRINGS dict.

`depth` is an optional integer to control the number of adjacent time
units returned.

Originally adapted from
https://web.archive.org/web/20060617175230/http://blog.natbat.co.uk/archive/2003/Jun/14/time_since
Modified to improve results for years and months.

functions.name: django.utils.timesince.timeuntil
documentation: Like timesince, but return a string measuring the time until the given time.

functions.name: django.utils.http.urlencode
documentation:
A version of Python's urllib.parse.urlencode() function that can operate on
MultiValueDict and non-string values.

functions.name: django.utils.http.http_date
documentation:
Format the time to match the RFC 5322 date format as specified by RFC 9110
Section 5.6.7.

`epoch_seconds` is a floating point number expressed in seconds since the
epoch, in UTC - such as that outputted by time.time(). If set to None, it
defaults to the current time.

Output a string in the format 'Wdy, DD Mon YYYY HH:MM:SS GMT'.

functions.name: django.utils.http.parse_http_date
documentation:
Parse a date format as specified by HTTP RFC 9110 Section 5.6.7.

The three formats allowed by the RFC are accepted, even if only the first
one is still in widespread use.

Return an integer expressed in seconds since the epoch, in UTC.

functions.name: django.utils.http.parse_http_date_safe
documentation: Same as parse_http_date, but return None if the input is invalid.

functions.name: django.utils.http.base36_to_int
documentation:
Convert a base 36 string to an int. Raise ValueError if the input won't fit
into an int.
Base 36 functions: useful for generating compact URLs

functions.name: django.utils.http.int_to_base36
documentation: Convert an integer to a base36 string.

functions.name: django.utils.http.urlsafe_base64_encode
documentation:
Encode a bytestring to a base64 string for use in URLs. Strip any trailing
equal signs.

functions.name: django.utils.http.urlsafe_base64_decode
documentation:
Decode a base64 encoded string. Add back any trailing equal signs that
might have been stripped.

functions.name: django.utils.http.parse_etags
documentation:
Parse a string of ETags given in an If-None-Match or If-Match header as
defined by RFC 9110. Return a list of quoted ETags, or ['*'] if all ETags
should be matched.

functions.name: django.utils.http.quote_etag
documentation:
If the provided string is already a quoted ETag, return it. Otherwise, wrap
the string in quotes, making it a strong ETag.

functions.name: django.utils.http.is_same_domain
documentation:
Return `True` if the host is either an exact match or a match
to the wildcard pattern.

Any pattern beginning with a period matches a domain and all of its
subdomains. (e.g. `.example.com` matches `example.com` and
`foo.example.com`). Anything else is an exact string match.

functions.name: django.utils.http.url_has_allowed_host_and_scheme
documentation:
Return `True` if the url uses an allowed host and a safe scheme.

Always return `False` on an empty url.

If `require_https` is `True`, only 'https' will be considered a valid
scheme, as opposed to 'http' and 'https' with the default, `False`.

Note: "True" doesn't entail that a URL is "safe". It may still be e.g.
quoted incorrectly. Ensure to also use django.utils.encoding.iri_to_uri()
on the path component of untrusted URLs.

functions.name: django.utils.http.escape_leading_slashes
documentation:
If redirecting to an absolute path (two leading slashes), a slash must be
escaped to prevent browsers from handling the path as schemaless and
redirecting to another host.

functions.name: django.utils.http.parse_header_parameters
documentation:
Parse a Content-type like header.
Return the main content-type and a dictionary of options.

functions.name: django.utils.http.content_disposition_header
documentation:
Construct a Content-Disposition HTTP header value from the given filename
as specified by RFC 6266.

functions.name: django.test.utils.instrumented_test_render
documentation:
An instrumented Template render method, providing a signal that can be
intercepted by the test Client.

functions.name: django.test.utils.setup_test_environment
documentation:
Perform global pre-test setup, such as installing the instrumented template
renderer and setting the email backend to the locmem email backend.

functions.name: django.test.utils.teardown_test_environment
documentation:
Perform any global post-test teardown, such as restoring the original
template renderer and restoring the email sending functions.

functions.name: django.test.utils.setup_databases
documentation: Create the test databases.

functions.name: django.test.utils.iter_test_cases
documentation:
Return an iterator over a test suite's unittest.TestCase objects.

The tests argument can also be an iterable of TestCase objects.

functions.name: django.test.utils.dependency_ordered
documentation:
Reorder test_databases into an order that honors the dependencies
described in TEST[DEPENDENCIES].

functions.name: django.test.utils.get_unique_databases_and_mirrors
documentation:
Figure out which databases actually need to be created.

Deduplicate entries in DATABASES that correspond the same database or are
configured as test mirrors.

Return two values:
- test_databases: ordered mapping of signatures to (name, list of aliases)
                  where all aliases share the same underlying database.
- mirrored_aliases: mapping of mirror aliases to original aliases.

functions.name: django.test.utils.teardown_databases
documentation: Destroy all the non-mirror databases.

functions.name: django.test.utils.compare_xml
documentation:
Try to do a 'xml-comparison' of want and got. Plain string comparison
doesn't always work because, for example, attribute ordering should not be
important. Ignore comment nodes, processing instructions, document type
node, and leading and trailing whitespaces.

Based on https://github.com/lxml/lxml/blob/master/src/lxml/doctestcompare.py

functions.name: django.test.utils.extend_sys_path
documentation: Context manager to temporarily add paths to sys.path.

functions.name: django.test.utils.isolate_lru_cache
documentation: Clear the cache of an LRU cache object on entering and exiting.

functions.name: django.test.utils.captured_output
documentation:
Return a context manager used by captured_stdout/stdin/stderr
that temporarily replaces the sys stream *stream_name* with a StringIO.

Note: This function and the following `captured_std*` are copied

: from CPython's `test.support` module.

functions.name: django.test.utils.captured_stdout
documentation:
Capture the output of sys.stdout:

    with captured_stdout() as stdout:
        print("hello")
    self.assertEqual(stdout.getvalue(), "hello
")

functions.name: django.test.utils.captured_stderr
documentation:
Capture the output of sys.stderr:

    with captured_stderr() as stderr:
        print("hello", file=sys.stderr)
    self.assertEqual(stderr.getvalue(), "hello
")

functions.name: django.test.utils.captured_stdin
documentation:
Capture the input to sys.stdin:

    with captured_stdin() as stdin:
        stdin.write('hello
')
        stdin.seek(0)
        # call test code that consumes from sys.stdin
        captured = input()
    self.assertEqual(captured, "hello")

functions.name: django.test.utils.freeze_time
documentation:
Context manager to temporarily freeze time.time(). This temporarily
modifies the time function of the time module. Modules which import the
time function directly (e.g. `from time import time`) won't be affected
This isn't meant as a public API, but helps reduce some repetitive code in
Django's test suite.

functions.name: django.test.utils.require_jinja2
documentation:
Decorator to enable a Jinja2 template engine in addition to the regular
Django template engine for a test or skip it if Jinja2 isn't available.

functions.name: django.test.utils.tag
documentation: Decorator to add tags to a test class or method.

functions.name: django.test.utils.register_lookup
documentation:
Context manager to temporarily register lookups on a model field using
lookup_name (or the lookup's lookup_name if not provided).

classes.name: django.test.utils.ContextList

methods.name: django.test.utils.ContextList.keys
documentation: Flattened keys of subcontexts.

A wrapper that provides direct key access to context items contained
in a list of context objects.

classes.name: django.test.utils.TestContextDecorator
documentation:
A base class that can either be used as a context manager during tests
or as a test function or unittest.TestCase subclass decorator to perform
temporary alterations.

`attr_name`: attribute assigned the return value of enable() if used as
             a class decorator.

`kwarg_name`: keyword argument passing the return value of enable() if
              used as a function decorator.

classes.name: django.test.utils.override_settings
documentation:
Act as either a decorator or a context manager. If it's a decorator, take a
function and return a wrapped function. If it's a contextmanager, use it
with the `with` statement. In either event, entering/exiting are called
before and after, respectively, the function/block is executed.

classes.name: django.test.utils.modify_settings
documentation:
Like override_settings, but makes it possible to append, prepend, or remove
items instead of redefining the entire list.

classes.name: django.test.utils.override_system_checks
documentation:
Act as a decorator. Override list of registered system checks.
Useful when you override `INSTALLED_APPS`, e.g. if you exclude `auth` app,
you also need to exclude its system checks.

classes.name: django.test.utils.CaptureQueriesContext
documentation: Context manager that captures queries executed by the specified connection.

classes.name: django.test.utils.override_script_prefix
documentation: Decorator or context manager to temporary override the script prefix.

classes.name: django.test.utils.LoggingCaptureMixin
documentation:
Capture the output from the 'django' logger and store it on the class's
logger_output attribute.

classes.name: django.test.utils.isolate_apps
documentation:
Act as either a decorator or a context manager to register models defined
in its wrapped context to an isolated registry.

The list of installed apps the isolated registry should contain must be
passed as arguments.

Two optional keyword arguments can be specified:

`attr_name`: attribute assigned the isolated registry if used as a class
             decorator.

`kwarg_name`: keyword argument passing the isolated registry if used as a
              function decorator.

modules.name: django.utils.xmlutils
documentation: Utilities for XML generation/parsing.

modules.name: django.db.models.query
documentation: The main QuerySet implementation. This provides the public API for the ORM.

functions.name: django.db.models.query.normalize_prefetch_lookups
documentation: Normalize lookups into Prefetch objects.

functions.name: django.db.models.query.prefetch_related_objects
documentation:
Populate prefetched object caches for a list of model instances based on
the lookups/Prefetch instances given.

functions.name: django.db.models.query.aprefetch_related_objects
documentation: See prefetch_related_objects().

functions.name: django.db.models.query.get_prefetcher
documentation:
For the attribute 'through_attr' on the given instance, find
an object that has a get_prefetch_querysets().
Return a 4 tuple containing:
(the object with get_prefetch_querysets (or None),
 the descriptor object representing this relationship (or None),
 a boolean that is False if the attribute was not found at all,
 a function that takes an instance and returns a boolean that is True if
 the attribute has already been fetched for that instance)

functions.name: django.db.models.query.prefetch_one_level
documentation:
Helper function for prefetch_related_objects().

Run prefetches on all instances using the prefetcher object,
assigning results to relevant caches in instance.

Return the prefetched objects along with any additional prefetches that
must be done due to prefetch_related lookups found from default managers.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.BaseIterable.__aiter__
documentation: __aiter__() is a *synchronous* method that has to then return an
*asynchronous* iterator/generator. Thus, nest an async generator inside
it.
This is a generic iterable converter for now, and is going to suffer a
performance penalty on large sets of items due to the cost of crossing
over the sync barrier for each chunk. Custom __aiter__() methods should
be added to each Iterable subclass, but that needs some work in the
Compiler first.

classes.name: django.db.models.query.ModelIterable
documentation: Iterable that yields a model instance for each row.

classes.name: django.db.models.query.RawModelIterable
documentation: Iterable that yields a model instance for each row from a raw queryset.

classes.name: django.db.models.query.ValuesIterable
documentation: Iterable returned by QuerySet.values() that yields a dict for each row.

classes.name: django.db.models.query.ValuesListIterable
documentation:
Iterable returned by QuerySet.values_list(flat=False) that yields a tuple
for each row.

classes.name: django.db.models.query.NamedValuesListIterable
documentation:
Iterable returned by QuerySet.values_list(named=True) that yields a
namedtuple for each row.

classes.name: django.db.models.query.FlatValuesListIterable
documentation:
Iterable returned by QuerySet.values_list(flat=True) that yields single
values.

classes.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.__deepcopy__
documentation: Don't populate the QuerySet's cache.
PYTHON MAGIC METHODS #

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.__iter__
documentation:
The queryset iterator protocol uses three nested iterators in the
default case:
    1. sql.compiler.execute_sql()
       - Returns 100 rows at time (constants.GET_ITERATOR_CHUNK_SIZE)
         using cursor.fetchmany(). This part is responsible for
         doing some column masking, and returning the rows in chunks.
    2. sql.compiler.results_iter()
       - Returns one row at time. At this point the rows are still just
         tuples. In some cases the return values are converted to
         Python values at this location.
    3. self.iterator()
       - Responsible for turning the rows into model objects.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.__getitem__
documentation: Retrieve an item or slice from the set of results.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet._iterator
documentation: METHODS THAT DO DATABASE QUERIES #

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.iterator
documentation:
An iterator over the results from applying this QuerySet to the
database. chunk_size must be provided for QuerySets that prefetch
related objects. Otherwise, a default chunk_size of 2000 is supplied.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.aiterator
documentation:
An asynchronous iterator over the results from applying this QuerySet
to the database.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.aggregate
documentation:
Return a dictionary containing the calculations (aggregation)
over the current queryset.

If args is present the expression is passed as a kwarg using
the Aggregate object's default alias.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.count
documentation:
Perform a SELECT COUNT() and return the number of records as an
integer.

If the QuerySet is already fully cached, return the length of the
cached results set to avoid multiple SELECT COUNT(*) calls.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.get
documentation:
Perform the query and return a single object matching the given
keyword arguments.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.create
documentation:
Create a new object with the given kwargs, saving it to the database
and returning the created object.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.bulk_create
documentation:
Insert each of the instances into the database. Do *not* call
save() on each of the instances, do not send any pre/post_save
signals, and do not set the primary key attribute if it is an
autoincrement field (except if features.can_return_rows_from_bulk_insert=True).
Multi-table models are not supported.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.bulk_update
documentation: Update the given fields in each of the given objects in the database.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.get_or_create
documentation:
Look up an object with the given kwargs, creating one if necessary.
Return a tuple of (object, created), where created is a boolean
specifying whether an object was created.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.update_or_create
documentation:
Look up an object with the given kwargs, updating one with defaults
if it exists, otherwise create a new one. Optionally, an object can
be created with different values than defaults by using
create_defaults.
Return a tuple (object, created), where created is a boolean
specifying whether an object was created.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet._extract_model_params
documentation:
Prepare `params` for creating a model instance based on the given
kwargs; for use by get_or_create().

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet._earliest
documentation:
Return the earliest object according to fields (if given) or by the
model's Meta.get_latest_by.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.latest
documentation:
Return the latest object according to fields (if given) or by the
model's Meta.get_latest_by.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.first
documentation: Return the first object of a query or None if no match is found.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.last
documentation: Return the last object of a query or None if no match is found.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.in_bulk
documentation:
Return a dictionary mapping each of the given IDs to the object with
that ID. If `id_list` isn't provided, evaluate the entire QuerySet.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.delete
documentation: Delete the records in the current QuerySet.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet._raw_delete
documentation:
Delete objects found from the given queryset in single direct SQL
query. No signals are sent and there is no protection for cascades.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.update
documentation:
Update all elements in the current QuerySet, setting all the given
fields to the appropriate values.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet._update
documentation:
A version of update() that accepts field objects instead of field names.
Used primarily for model saving and not intended for use by general
code (it requires too much poking around at model internals to be
useful at that level).

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.exists
documentation: Return True if the QuerySet would have any results, False otherwise.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.contains
documentation:
Return True if the QuerySet contains the provided obj,
False otherwise.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.explain
documentation:
Runs an EXPLAIN on the SQL query this QuerySet would perform, and
returns the results.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.raw
documentation: PUBLIC METHODS THAT RETURN A QUERYSET SUBCLASS #

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.dates
documentation:
Return a list of date objects representing all available dates for
the given field_name, scoped to 'kind'.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.datetimes
documentation:
Return a list of datetime objects representing all available
datetimes for the given field_name, scoped to 'kind'.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.none
documentation: Return an empty QuerySet.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.all
documentation:
Return a new QuerySet that is a copy of the current one. This allows a
QuerySet to proxy for a model manager in some cases.
PUBLIC METHODS THAT ALTER ATTRIBUTES AND RETURN A NEW QUERYSET #

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.filter
documentation:
Return a new QuerySet instance with the args ANDed to the existing
set.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.exclude
documentation:
Return a new QuerySet instance with NOT (args) ANDed to the existing
set.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.complex_filter
documentation:
Return a new QuerySet instance with filter_obj added to the filters.

filter_obj can be a Q object or a dictionary of keyword lookup
arguments.

This exists to support framework features such as 'limit_choices_to',
and usually it will be more natural to use other methods.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.select_for_update
documentation:
Return a new QuerySet instance that will select objects with a
FOR UPDATE lock.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.select_related
documentation:
Return a new QuerySet instance that will select related objects.

If fields are specified, they must be ForeignKey fields and only those
related objects are included in the selection.

If select_related(None) is called, clear the list.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.prefetch_related
documentation:
Return a new QuerySet instance that will prefetch the specified
Many-To-One and Many-To-Many related objects when the QuerySet is
evaluated.

When prefetch_related() is called more than once, append to the list of
prefetch lookups. If prefetch_related(None) is called, clear the list.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.annotate
documentation:
Return a query set in which the returned objects have been annotated
with extra data or aggregations.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.alias
documentation: Return a query set with added aliases for extra data or aggregations.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.order_by
documentation: Return a new QuerySet instance with the ordering changed.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.distinct
documentation: Return a new QuerySet instance that will select only distinct results.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.extra
documentation: Add extra SQL fragments to the query.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.reverse
documentation: Reverse the ordering of the QuerySet.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.defer
documentation:
Defer the loading of data for certain fields until they are accessed.
Add the set of deferred fields to any existing set of deferred fields.
The only exception to this is if None is passed in as the only
parameter, in which case removal all deferrals.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.only
documentation:
Essentially, the opposite of defer(). Only the fields passed into this
method and that are not already specified as deferred are loaded
immediately when the queryset is evaluated.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.using
documentation: Select which database this QuerySet should execute against.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.ordered
documentation:
Return True if the QuerySet is ordered -- i.e. has an order_by()
clause or a default ordering on the model (or is empty).
PUBLIC INTROSPECTION ATTRIBUTES #

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet.db
documentation: Return the database used if this query is executed now.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet._insert
documentation:
Insert a new record for the given model. This provides an interface to
the InsertQuery class and is how Model.save() is implemented.
PRIVATE METHODS #

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet._batched_insert
documentation: Helper method for bulk_create() to insert objs one batch at a time.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet._chain
documentation:
Return a copy of the current QuerySet that's ready for another
operation.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet._clone
documentation:
Return a copy of the current QuerySet. A lightweight alternative
to deepcopy().

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet._next_is_sticky
documentation:
Indicate that the next filter call and the one following that should
be treated as a single filter. This is only important when it comes to
determining when to reuse tables for many-to-many filters. Required so
that we can filter naturally on the results of related managers.

This doesn't return a clone of the current QuerySet (it returns
"self"). The method is only used internally and should be immediately
followed by a filter() that does create a clone.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet._merge_sanity_check
documentation: Check that two QuerySet classes may be merged.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet._merge_known_related_objects
documentation: Keep track of all known related objects from either QuerySet instance.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet._add_hints
documentation:
Update hinting information for use by routers. Add new key/values or
overwrite existing key/values.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.QuerySet._has_filters
documentation:
Check if this QuerySet has any filtering going on. This isn't
equivalent with checking if all objects are present in results, for
example, qs[1:]._has_filters() -> False.
Represent a lazy database lookup for a set of objects.

classes.name: django.db.models.query.EmptyQuerySet
documentation:
Marker class to checking if a queryset is empty by .none():
    isinstance(qs.none(), EmptyQuerySet) -> True

classes.name: django.db.models.query.RawQuerySet

methods.name: django.db.models.query.RawQuerySet.resolve_model_init_order
documentation: Resolve the init field names and value positions.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.RawQuerySet.prefetch_related
documentation: Same as QuerySet.prefetch_related()

methods.name: django.db.models.query.RawQuerySet._clone
documentation: Same as QuerySet._clone()

methods.name: django.db.models.query.RawQuerySet.db
documentation: Return the database used if this query is executed now.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.RawQuerySet.using
documentation: Select the database this RawQuerySet should execute against.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.RawQuerySet.columns
documentation:
A list of model field names in the order they'll appear in the
query results.

methods.name: django.db.models.query.RawQuerySet.model_fields
documentation: A dict mapping column names to model field names.

Provide an iterator which converts the results of raw SQL queries into
annotated model instances.

classes.name: django.db.models.query.RelatedPopulator
documentation:
RelatedPopulator is used for select_related() object instantiation.

The idea is that each select_related() model will be populated by a
different RelatedPopulator instance. The RelatedPopulator instances get
klass_info and select (computed in SQLCompiler) plus the used db as
input for initialization. That data is used to compute which columns
to use, how to instantiate the model, and how to populate the links
between the objects.

The actual creation of the objects is done in populate() method. This
method gets row and from_obj as input and populates the select_related()
model instance.

modules.name: django.urls.resolvers
documentation:
This module converts requested URLs to callback view functions.

URLResolver is the main class here. Its resolve() method takes a URL (as
a string) and returns a ResolverMatch object which provides access to all
attributes of the resolved URL match.

functions.name: django.urls.resolvers._route_to_regex
documentation:
Convert a path pattern into a regular expression. Return the regular
expression and a dictionary mapping the capture names to the converters.
For example, 'foo/<int:pk>' returns '^foo\/(?P<pk>[0-9]+)'
and {'pk': <django.urls.converters.IntConverter>}.

methods.name: django.urls.resolvers.LocaleRegexDescriptor.__get__
documentation: Return a compiled regular expression based on the active language.

methods.name: django.urls.resolvers.CheckURLMixin.describe
documentation: Format the URL pattern for display in warning messages.

methods.name: django.urls.resolvers.CheckURLMixin._check_pattern_startswith_slash
documentation: Check that the pattern does not begin with a forward slash.

methods.name: django.urls.resolvers.LocaleRegexRouteDescriptor.__get__
documentation: Return a compiled regular expression based on the active language.

methods.name: django.urls.resolvers.URLPattern._check_pattern_name
documentation: Check that the pattern name does not contain a colon.

methods.name: django.urls.resolvers.URLPattern.lookup_str
documentation:
A string that identifies the view (e.g. 'path.to.view_function' or
'path.to.ClassBasedView').

methods.name: django.urls.resolvers.URLResolver._join_route
documentation: Join two routes, without the starting ^ in the second route.

functions.name: django.utils.version.get_version
documentation: Return a PEP 440-compliant version number from VERSION.

functions.name: django.utils.version.get_main_version
documentation: Return main version (X.Y[.Z]) from VERSION.

functions.name: django.utils.version.get_complete_version
documentation:
Return a tuple of the django version. If version argument is non-empty,
check for correctness of the tuple provided.

functions.name: django.utils.version.get_git_changeset
documentation:
Return a numeric identifier of the latest git changeset.

The result is the UTC timestamp of the changeset in YYYYMMDDHHMMSS format.
This value isn't guaranteed to be unique, but collisions are very unlikely,
so it's sufficient for generating the development version numbers.

functions.name: django.utils.version.get_version_tuple
documentation:
Return a tuple of version numbers (e.g. (1, 2, 3)) from the version
string (e.g. '1.2.3').

modules.name: django.utils.tree
documentation:
A class for storing a tree graph. Primarily used for filter constructs in the
ORM.

classes.name: django.utils.tree.Node

methods.name: django.utils.tree.Node.__init__
documentation: Construct a new Node. If no connector is given, use the default.

methods.name: django.utils.tree.Node.create
documentation:
Create a new instance using Node() instead of __init__() as some
subclasses, e.g. django.db.models.query_utils.Q, may implement a custom
__init__() with a signature that conflicts with the one defined in
Node.__init__().

methods.name: django.utils.tree.Node.__len__
documentation: Return the number of children this node has.

methods.name: django.utils.tree.Node.__bool__
documentation: Return whether or not this node has children.

methods.name: django.utils.tree.Node.__contains__
documentation: Return True if 'other' is a direct child of this instance.

methods.name: django.utils.tree.Node.add
documentation:
Combine this tree and the data represented by data using the
connector conn_type. The combine is done by squashing the node other
away if possible.

This tree (self) will never be pushed to a child node of the
combined tree, nor will the connector or negated properties change.

Return a node which can be used in place of data regardless if the
node other got squashed or not.

methods.name: django.utils.tree.Node.negate
documentation: Negate the sense of the root connector.

A single internal node in the tree graph. A Node should be viewed as a
connection (the root) with the children being either leaf nodes or other
Node instances.

modules.name: django.utils.regex_helper
documentation:
Functions for reversing a regular expression (used in reverse URL resolving).
Used internally by Django and not intended for external use.

This is not, and is not intended to be, a complete reg-exp decompiler. It
should be good enough for a large class of URLS, however.

functions.name: django.utils.regex_helper.normalize
documentation:
Given a reg-exp pattern, normalize it to an iterable of forms that
suffice for reverse matching. This does the following:

(1) For any repeating sections, keeps the minimum number of occurrences
    permitted (this means zero for optional groups).
(2) If an optional group includes parameters, include one occurrence of
    that group (along with the zero occurrence case from step (1)).
(3) Select the first (essentially an arbitrary) element from any character
    class. Select an arbitrary character for any unordered class (e.g. '.'
    or '\w') in the pattern.
(4) Ignore look-ahead and look-behind assertions.
(5) Raise an error on any disjunctive ('|') constructs.

Django's URLs for forward resolving are either all positional arguments or
all keyword arguments. That is assumed here, as well. Although reverse
resolving can be done using positional args when keyword args are
specified, the two cannot be mixed in the same reverse() call.

functions.name: django.utils.regex_helper.next_char
documentation:
An iterator that yields the next character from "pattern_iter", respecting
escape sequences. An escaped character is replaced by a representative of
its class (e.g. \w -> "x"). If the escaped character is one that is
skipped, it is not returned (the next character is returned instead).

Yield the next character, along with a boolean indicating whether it is a
raw (unescaped) character or not.

functions.name: django.utils.regex_helper.walk_to_end
documentation:
The iterator is currently inside a capturing group. Walk to the close of
this group, skipping over any nested groups and handling escaped
parentheses correctly.

functions.name: django.utils.regex_helper.get_quantifier
documentation:
Parse a quantifier from the input, where "ch" is the first character in the
quantifier.

Return the minimum number of occurrences permitted by the quantifier and
either None or the next character from the input_iter if the next character
is not part of the quantifier.

functions.name: django.utils.regex_helper.contains
documentation:
Return True if the "source" contains an instance of "inst". False,
otherwise.

functions.name: django.utils.regex_helper.flatten_result
documentation:
Turn the given source sequence into a list of reg-exp possibilities and
their arguments. Return a list of strings and a list of argument lists.
Each of the two lists will be of the same length.

functions.name: django.utils.regex_helper._lazy_re_compile
documentation: Lazily compile a regex with flags.

classes.name: django.utils.regex_helper.Choice
documentation: Represent multiple possibilities at this point in a pattern string.

classes.name: django.utils.regex_helper.Group
documentation: Represent a capturing group in the pattern string.

classes.name: django.utils.regex_helper.NonCapture
documentation: Represent a non-capturing group in the pattern string.

functions.name: django.views.decorators.common.no_append_slash
documentation:
Mark a view function as excluded from CommonMiddleware's APPEND_SLASH
redirection.

modules.name: django.db.models.sql.query
documentation:
Create SQL statements for QuerySets.

The code in here encapsulates all of the SQL construction so that QuerySets
themselves do not have to (and could be backed by things other than SQL
databases). The abstraction barrier only works one way: this module has to know
all about the internals of models in order to get the information it needs.

functions.name: django.db.models.sql.query.get_order_dir
documentation:
Return the field name and direction for an order specification. For
example, '-foo' is returned as ('foo', 'DESC').

The 'default' param is used to indicate which way no prefix (or a '+'
prefix) should sort. The '-' prefix always sorts the opposite way.

classes.name: django.db.models.sql.query.RawQuery
documentation: A single raw SQL query.

classes.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.__str__
documentation:
Return the query as a string of SQL with the parameter values
substituted in (use sql_with_params() to see the unsubstituted string).

Parameter values won't necessarily be quoted correctly, since that is
done by the database interface at execution time.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.sql_with_params
documentation:
Return the query as an SQL string and the parameters that will be
substituted into the query.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.__deepcopy__
documentation: Limit the amount of work when a Query is deepcopied.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.get_meta
documentation:
Return the Options instance (the model._meta) from which to start
processing. Normally, this is self.model._meta, but it can be changed
by subclasses.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.clone
documentation:
Return a copy of the current Query. A lightweight alternative to
deepcopy().

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.chain
documentation:
Return a copy of the current Query that's ready for another operation.
The klass argument changes the type of the Query, e.g. UpdateQuery.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.get_aggregation
documentation: Return the dictionary with the values of the existing aggregations.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.get_count
documentation: Perform a COUNT() query using the current filter constraints.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.combine
documentation:
Merge the 'rhs' query into the current one (with any 'rhs' effects
being applied *after* (that is, "to the right of") anything in the
current query. 'rhs' is not modified during a call to this function.

The 'connector' parameter describes how to connect filters from the
'rhs' query.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.get_select_mask
documentation:
Convert the self.deferred_loading data structure to an alternate data
structure, describing the field that *will* be loaded. This is used to
compute the columns to select from the database and also by the
QuerySet class to work out which fields are being initialized on each
model. Models that have all their fields included aren't mentioned in
the result, only those that have field restrictions in place.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.table_alias
documentation:
Return a table alias for the given table_name and whether this is a
new alias or not.

If 'create' is true, a new alias is always created. Otherwise, the
most recently created alias for the table (if one exists) is reused.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.ref_alias
documentation: Increases the reference count for this alias.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.unref_alias
documentation: Decreases the reference count for this alias.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.promote_joins
documentation:
Promote recursively the join type of given aliases and its children to
an outer join. If 'unconditional' is False, only promote the join if
it is nullable or the parent join is an outer join.

The children promotion is done to avoid join chains that contain a LOUTER
b INNER c. So, if we have currently a INNER b INNER c and a->b is promoted,
then we must also promote b->c automatically, or otherwise the promotion
of a->b doesn't actually change anything in the query results.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.demote_joins
documentation:
Change join type from LOUTER to INNER for all joins in aliases.

Similarly to promote_joins(), this method must ensure no join chains
containing first an outer, then an inner join are generated. If we
are demoting b->c join in chain a LOUTER b LOUTER c then we must
demote a->b automatically, or otherwise the demotion of b->c doesn't
actually change anything in the query results. .

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.reset_refcounts
documentation:
Reset reference counts for aliases so that they match the value passed
in `to_counts`.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.change_aliases
documentation:
Change the aliases in change_map (which maps old-alias -> new-alias),
relabelling any references to them in select columns and the where
clause.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.bump_prefix
documentation:
Change the alias prefix to the next letter in the alphabet in a way
that the other query's aliases and this query's aliases will not
conflict. Even tables that previously had no alias will get an alias
after this call. To prevent changing aliases use the exclude parameter.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.get_initial_alias
documentation:
Return the first alias for this query, after increasing its reference
count.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.count_active_tables
documentation:
Return the number of tables in this query with a non-zero reference
count. After execution, the reference counts are zeroed, so tables
added in compiler will not be seen by this method.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.join
documentation:
Return an alias for the 'join', either reusing an existing alias for
that join or creating a new one. 'join' is either a base_table_class or
join_class.

The 'reuse' parameter can be either None which means all joins are
reusable, or it can be a set containing the aliases that can be reused.

A join is always created as LOUTER if the lhs alias is LOUTER to make
sure chains like t1 LOUTER t2 INNER t3 aren't generated. All new
joins are created as LOUTER if the join is nullable.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.join_parent_model
documentation:
Make sure the given 'model' is joined in the query. If 'model' isn't
a parent of 'opts' or if it is None this method is a no-op.

The 'alias' is the root alias for starting the join, 'seen' is a dict
of model -> alias of existing joins. It must also contain a mapping
of None -> some alias. This will be returned in the no-op case.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.add_annotation
documentation: Add a single annotation expression to the Query.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.solve_lookup_type
documentation: Solve the lookup type from the lookup (e.g.: 'foobar__id__icontains').

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.check_query_object_type
documentation:
Check whether the object passed while querying is of the correct type.
If not, raise a ValueError specifying the wrong object.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.check_related_objects
documentation: Check the type of object passed to query relations.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.check_filterable
documentation: Raise an error if expression cannot be used in a WHERE clause.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.build_lookup
documentation:
Try to extract transforms and lookup from given lhs.

The lhs value is something that works like SQLExpression.
The rhs value is what the lookup is going to compare against.
The lookups is a list of names to extract using get_lookup()
and get_transform().

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.try_transform
documentation:
Helper method for build_lookup(). Try to fetch and initialize
a transform for name parameter from lhs.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.build_filter
documentation:
Build a WhereNode for a single filter clause but don't add it
to this Query. Query.add_q() will then add this filter to the where
Node.

The 'branch_negated' tells us if the current branch contains any
negations. This will be used to determine if subqueries are needed.

The 'current_negated' is used to determine if the current filter is
negated or not and this will be used to determine if IS NULL filtering
is needed.

The difference between current_negated and branch_negated is that
branch_negated is set on first negation, but current_negated is
flipped for each negation.

Note that add_filter will not do any negating itself, that is done
upper in the code by add_q().

The 'can_reuse' is a set of reusable joins for multijoins.

The method will create a filter clause that can be added to the current
query. However, if the filter isn't added to the query then the caller
is responsible for unreffing the joins used.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.add_q
documentation:
A preprocessor for the internal _add_q(). Responsible for doing final
join promotion.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query._add_q
documentation: Add a Q-object to the current filter.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.names_to_path
documentation:
Walk the list of names and turns them into PathInfo tuples. A single
name in 'names' can generate multiple PathInfos (m2m, for example).

'names' is the path of names to travel, 'opts' is the model Options we
start the name resolving from, 'allow_many' is as for setup_joins().
If fail_on_missing is set to True, then a name that can't be resolved
will generate a FieldError.

Return a list of PathInfo tuples. In addition return the final field
(the last used join field) and target (which is a field guaranteed to
contain the same value as the final field). Finally, return those names
that weren't found (which are likely transforms and the final lookup).

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.setup_joins
documentation:
Compute the necessary table joins for the passage through the fields
given in 'names'. 'opts' is the Options class for the current model
(which gives the table we are starting from), 'alias' is the alias for
the table to start the joining from.

The 'can_reuse' defines the reverse foreign key joins we can reuse. It
can be None in which case all joins are reusable or a set of aliases
that can be reused. Note that non-reverse foreign keys are always
reusable when using setup_joins().

If 'allow_many' is False, then any reverse foreign key seen will
generate a MultiJoin exception.

Return the final field involved in the joins, the target field (used
for any 'where' constraint), the final 'opts' value, the joins, the
field path traveled to generate the joins, and a transform function
that takes a field and alias and is equivalent to `field.get_col(alias)`
in the simple case but wraps field transforms if they were included in
names.

The target field is the field containing the concrete value. Final
field can be something different, for example foreign key pointing to
that value. Final field is needed for example in some value
conversions (convert 'obj' in fk__id=obj to pk val using the foreign
key field for example).

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.trim_joins
documentation:
The 'target' parameter is the final field being joined to, 'joins'
is the full list of join aliases. The 'path' contain the PathInfos
used to create the joins.

Return the final target field and table alias and the new active
joins.

Always trim any direct join if the target column is already in the
previous table. Can't trim reverse joins as it's unknown if there's
anything on the other side of the join.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.split_exclude
documentation:
When doing an exclude against any kind of N-to-many relation, we need
to use a subquery. This method constructs the nested query, given the
original exclude filter (filter_expr) and the portion up to the first
N-to-many relation field.

For example, if the origin filter is ~Q(child__name='foo'), filter_expr
is ('child__name', 'foo') and can_reuse is a set of joins usable for
filters in the original query.

We will turn this into equivalent of:
    WHERE NOT EXISTS(
        SELECT 1
        FROM child
        WHERE name = 'foo' AND child.parent_id = parent.id
        LIMIT 1
    )

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.set_limits
documentation:
Adjust the limits on the rows retrieved. Use low/high to set these,
as it makes it more Pythonic to read and write. When the SQL query is
created, convert them to the appropriate offset and limit values.

Apply any limits passed in here to the existing constraints. Add low
to the current low value and clamp both to any existing high value.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.clear_limits
documentation: Clear any existing limits.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.can_filter
documentation:
Return True if adding filters to this instance is still possible.

Typically, this means no limits or offsets have been put on the results.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.clear_select_clause
documentation: Remove all fields from SELECT clause.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.clear_select_fields
documentation:
Clear the list of fields to select (but not extra_select columns).
Some queryset types completely replace any existing list of select
columns.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.add_distinct_fields
documentation: Add and resolve the given fields to the query's "distinct on" clause.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.add_fields
documentation:
Add the given (model) fields to the select set. Add the field names in
the order specified.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.add_ordering
documentation:
Add items from the 'ordering' sequence to the query's "order by"
clause. These items are either field names (not column names) --
possibly with a direction prefix ('-' or '?') -- or OrderBy
expressions.

If 'ordering' is empty, clear all ordering from the query.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.clear_ordering
documentation:
Remove any ordering settings if the current query allows it without
side effects, set 'force' to True to clear the ordering regardless.
If 'clear_default' is True, there will be no ordering in the resulting
query (not even the model's default).

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.set_group_by
documentation:
Expand the GROUP BY clause required by the query.

This will usually be the set of all non-aggregate fields in the
return data. If the database backend supports grouping by the
primary key, and the query would be equivalent, the optimization
will be made automatically.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.add_select_related
documentation:
Set up the select_related data structure so that we only select
certain related models (as opposed to all models, when
self.select_related=True).

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.add_extra
documentation:
Add data to the various extra_* attributes for user-created additions
to the query.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.clear_deferred_loading
documentation: Remove any fields from the deferred loading set.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.add_deferred_loading
documentation:
Add the given list of model field names to the set of fields to
exclude from loading from the database when automatic column selection
is done. Add the new field names to any existing field names that
are deferred (or removed from any existing field names that are marked
as the only ones for immediate loading).

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.add_immediate_loading
documentation:
Add the given list of model field names to the set of fields to
retrieve when the SQL is executed ("immediate loading" fields). The
field names replace any existing immediate loading field names. If
there are field names already specified for deferred loading, remove
those names from the new field_names before storing the new names
for immediate loading. (That is, immediate loading overrides any
existing immediate values, but respects existing deferrals.)

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.set_annotation_mask
documentation: Set the mask of annotations that will be returned by the SELECT.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.set_extra_mask
documentation:
Set the mask of extra select items that will be returned by SELECT.
Don't remove them from the Query since they might be used later.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.annotation_select
documentation:
Return the dictionary of aggregate columns that are not masked and
should be used in the SELECT clause. Cache this result for performance.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.trim_start
documentation:
Trim joins from the start of the join path. The candidates for trim
are the PathInfos in names_with_path structure that are m2m joins.

Also set the select column so the start matches the join.

This method is meant to be used for generating the subquery joins &
cols in split_exclude().

Return a lookup usable for doing outerq.filter(lookup=self) and a
boolean indicating if the joins in the prefix contain a LEFT OUTER join.
_

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.is_nullable
documentation:
Check if the given field should be treated as nullable.

Some backends treat '' as null and Django treats such fields as
nullable for those backends. In such situations field.null can be
False even if we should treat the field as nullable.

class_variables.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.low_mark
documentation: Used for offset/limit.

class_variables.name: django.db.models.sql.query.Query.high_mark
documentation: Used for offset/limit.
A single SQL query.

classes.name: django.db.models.sql.query.JoinPromoter

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.JoinPromoter.add_votes
documentation:
Add single vote per item to self.votes. Parameter can be any
iterable.

methods.name: django.db.models.sql.query.JoinPromoter.update_join_types
documentation:
Change join types so that the generated query is as efficient as
possible, but still correct. So, change as many joins as possible
to INNER, but don't make OUTER joins INNER if that could remove
results from the query.

A class to abstract away join promotion problems for complex filter
conditions.

modules.name: django.utils.html
documentation: HTML utilities suitable for global use.

functions.name: django.utils.html.escape
documentation:
Return the given text with ampersands, quotes and angle brackets encoded
for use in HTML.

Always escape input, even if it's already escaped and marked as such.
This may result in double-escaping. If this is a concern, use
conditional_escape() instead.

functions.name: django.utils.html.escapejs
documentation: Hex encode characters for use in JavaScript strings.

functions.name: django.utils.html.json_script
documentation:
Escape all the HTML/XML special characters with their unicode escapes, so
value is safe to be output anywhere except for inside a tag attribute. Wrap
the escaped JSON in a script tag.

functions.name: django.utils.html.conditional_escape
documentation:
Similar to escape(), except that it doesn't operate on pre-escaped strings.

This function relies on the __html__ convention used both by Django's
SafeData class and by third-party libraries like markupsafe.

functions.name: django.utils.html.format_html
documentation:
Similar to str.format, but pass all arguments through conditional_escape(),
and call mark_safe() on the result. This function should be used instead
of str.format or % interpolation to build up small HTML fragments.

functions.name: django.utils.html.format_html_join
documentation:
A wrapper of format_html, for the common case of a group of arguments that need to be formatted using the same format string, and then joined using 'sep'. 'sep' is also passed through conditional_escape. 'args_generator' should be an iterator that returns the sequence of 'args' that will be passed to format_html. Example: format_html_join(' ', "
  • {} {}
    • ", ((u.first_name, u.last_name) for u in users))

functions.name: django.utils.html.linebreaks
documentation:
Convert newlines into

and
s.

functions.name: django.utils.html._strip_once
documentation: Internal tag stripping utility used by strip_tags.

functions.name: django.utils.html.strip_tags
documentation: Return the given HTML with all tags stripped.

functions.name: django.utils.html.strip_spaces_between_tags
documentation: Return the given HTML with spaces between tags removed.

functions.name: django.utils.html.smart_urlquote
documentation: Quote a URL if it isn't already quoted.

functions.name: django.utils.html.avoid_wrapping
documentation:
Avoid text wrapping in the middle of a phrase by adding non-breaking
spaces where there previously were normal spaces.

functions.name: django.utils.html.html_safe
documentation:
A decorator that defines the __html__ method. This helps non-Django
templates to detect classes whose __str__ methods return SafeString.

classes.name: django.utils.html.Urlizer

methods.name: django.utils.html.Urlizer.__call__
documentation:
If trim_url_limit is not None, truncate the URLs in the link text
longer than this limit to trim_url_limit - 1 characters and append an
ellipsis.

If nofollow is True, give the links a rel="nofollow" attribute.

If autoescape is True, autoescape the link text and URLs.

methods.name: django.utils.html.Urlizer.trim_punctuation
documentation:
Trim trailing and wrapping punctuation from `word`. Return the items of
the new state.

methods.name: django.utils.html.Urlizer.is_email_simple
documentation: Return True if value looks like an email address.

Convert any URLs in text into clickable links.

Work on http://, https://, www. links, and also on links ending in one of
the original seven gTLDs (.com, .edu, .gov, .int, .mil, .net, and .org).
Links can have trailing punctuation (periods, commas, close-parens) and
leading punctuation (opening parens) and it'll still do the right thing.

modules.name: django.utils.timezone
documentation: Timezone-related classes and functions.

functions.name: django.utils.timezone.get_fixed_timezone
documentation: Return a tzinfo instance with a fixed offset from UTC.

functions.name: django.utils.timezone.get_default_timezone
documentation:
Return the default time zone as a tzinfo instance.

This is the time zone defined by settings.TIME_ZONE.
In order to avoid accessing settings at compile time,
wrap the logic in a function and cache the result.

These functions don't change os.environ['TZ'] and call time.tzset()
because it isn't thread safe.

If use_tz is provided and is not None, that will force the value to

This function is designed for use by the template engine.
Templates

functions.name: django.utils.timezone.localtime
documentation:
Convert an aware datetime.datetime to local time.

Only aware datetimes are allowed. When value is omitted, it defaults to
now().

Local time is defined by the current time zone, unless another time zone
is specified.
Utilities

functions.name: django.utils.timezone.localdate
documentation:
Convert an aware datetime to local time and return the value's date.

Only aware datetimes are allowed. When value is omitted, it defaults to

Local time is defined by the current time zone, unless another time zone is

functions.name: django.utils.timezone.now
documentation: Return an aware or naive datetime.datetime, depending on settings.USE_TZ.

functions.name: django.utils.timezone.is_aware
documentation:
Determine if a given datetime.datetime is aware.

The concept is defined in Python's docs:
https://docs.python.org/library/datetime.html#datetime.tzinfo

Assuming value.tzinfo is either None or a proper datetime.tzinfo,
value.utcoffset() implements the appropriate logic.
By design, these four functions don't perform any checks on their arguments.
The caller should ensure that they don't receive an invalid value like None.

functions.name: django.utils.timezone.is_naive
documentation:
Determine if a given datetime.datetime is naive.

The concept is defined in Python's docs:
https://docs.python.org/library/datetime.html#datetime.tzinfo

Assuming value.tzinfo is either None or a proper datetime.tzinfo,
value.utcoffset() implements the appropriate logic.

functions.name: django.utils.timezone.make_naive
documentation: Make an aware datetime.datetime naive in a given time zone.

classes.name: django.utils.timezone.override
documentation:
Temporarily set the time zone for the current thread.

This is a context manager that uses django.utils.timezone.activate()
to set the timezone on entry and restores the previously active timezone
on exit.

The `timezone` argument must be an instance of a `tzinfo` subclass, a
time zone name, or `None`. If it is `None`, Django enables the default
time zone.

functions.name: django.views.decorators.cache.cache_page
documentation:
Decorator for views that tries getting the page from the cache and
populates the cache if the page isn't in the cache yet.

The cache is keyed by the URL and some data from the headers.
Additionally there is the key prefix that is used to distinguish different
cache areas in a multi-site setup. You could use the
get_current_site().domain, for example, as that is unique across a Django
project.

Additionally, all headers from the response's Vary header will be taken
into account on caching -- just like the middleware does.

functions.name: django.test.runner.shuffle_tests
documentation:
Return an iterator over the given tests in a shuffled order, keeping tests
next to other tests of their class.

`tests` should be an iterable of tests.

functions.name: django.test.runner.reorder_test_bin
documentation:
Return an iterator that reorders the given tests, keeping tests next to
other tests of their class.

`tests` should be an iterable of tests that supports reversed().

functions.name: django.test.runner.reorder_tests
documentation:
Reorder an iterable of tests, grouping by the given TestCase classes.

This function also removes any duplicates and reorders so that tests of the
same type are consecutive.

The result is returned as an iterator. `classes` is a sequence of types.
Tests that are instances of `classes[0]` are grouped first, followed by
instances of `classes[1]`, etc. Tests that are not instances of any of the
classes are grouped last.

If `reverse` is True, the tests within each `classes` group are reversed,
but without reversing the order of `classes` itself.

The `shuffler` argument is an optional instance of this module's `Shuffler`
class. If provided, tests will be shuffled within each `classes` group, but
keeping tests with other tests of their TestCase class. Reversing is
applied after shuffling to allow reversing the same random order.

functions.name: django.test.runner.partition_suite_by_case
documentation: Partition a test suite by TestCase, preserving the order of tests.

methods.name: django.test.runner.RemoteTestResult.wasSuccessful

classes.name: django.test.runner.RemoteTestRunner
documentation:
Run tests and record everything but don't display anything.

The implementation matches the unpythonic coding style of unittest2.

classes.name: django.test.runner.ParallelTestSuite

methods.name: django.test.runner.ParallelTestSuite.run
documentation:
Distribute TestCases across workers.

Return an identifier of each TestCase with its result in order to use
imap_unordered to show results as soon as they're available.

To minimize pickling errors when getting results from workers:

- pass back numeric indexes in self.subsuites instead of tests
- make tracebacks picklable with tblib, if available

Even with tblib, errors may still occur for dynamically created
exception classes which cannot be unpickled.

Run a series of tests in parallel in several processes.

While the unittest module's documentation implies that orchestrating the
execution of tests is the responsibility of the test runner, in practice,
it appears that TestRunner classes are more concerned with formatting and
displaying test results.

Since there are fewer use cases for customizing TestSuite than TestRunner,
implementing parallelization at the level of the TestSuite improves
interoperability with existing custom test runners. A single instance of a
test runner can still collect results from all tests without being aware
that they have been run in parallel.

classes.name: django.test.runner.Shuffler

methods.name: django.test.runner.Shuffler.shuffle
documentation:
Return a new list of the items in a shuffled order.

The `key` is a function that accepts an item in `items` and returns
a string unique for that item that can be viewed as a string id. The
order of the return value is deterministic. It depends on the seed
and key function but not on the original order.

This class implements shuffling with a special consistency property.
Consistency means that, for a given seed and key function, if two sets of
items are shuffled, the resulting order will agree on the intersection of
the two sets. For example, if items are removed from an original set, the
shuffled order for the new set will be the shuffled order of the original
set restricted to the smaller set.

functions.name: django.views.decorators.debug.sensitive_variables
documentation:
Indicate which variables used in the decorated function are sensitive so
that those variables can later be treated in a special way, for example
by hiding them when logging unhandled exceptions.

Accept two forms:

* with specified variable names:

    @sensitive_variables('user', 'password', 'credit_card')
    def my_function(user):
        password = user.pass_word
        credit_card = user.credit_card_number
        ...

* without any specified variable names, in which case consider all
  variables are sensitive:

    @sensitive_variables()
    def my_function()
        ...

functions.name: django.views.decorators.debug.sensitive_post_parameters
documentation:
Indicate which POST parameters used in the decorated view are sensitive,
so that those parameters can later be treated in a special way, for example
by hiding them when logging unhandled exceptions.

Accept two forms:

* with specified parameters:

    @sensitive_post_parameters('password', 'credit_card')
    def my_view(request):
        pw = request.POST['password']
        cc = request.POST['credit_card']
        ...

* without any specified parameters, in which case consider all
  variables are sensitive:

    @sensitive_post_parameters()
    def my_view(request)
        ...

modules.name: django.utils.feedgenerator
documentation:
Syndication feed generation library -- used for generating RSS, etc.

Sample usage:

```pycon
>>> from django.utils import feedgenerator
>>> feed = feedgenerator.Rss201rev2Feed(
...     title="Poynter E-Media Tidbits",
...     link="http://www.poynter.org/column.asp?id=31",
...     description="A group blog by the sharpest minds in online journalism.",
...     language="en",
... )
>>> feed.add_item(
...     title="Hello",
...     link="http://www.holovaty.com/test/",
...     description="Testing."
... )
>>> with open('test.rss', 'w') as fp:
...     feed.write(fp, 'utf-8')
```

For definitions of the different versions of RSS, see:
<https://web.archive.org/web/20110718035220/http://diveintomark.org/archives/2004/02/04/incompatible-rss>

modules.name: django.views.decorators.http
documentation: Decorators for views based on HTTP headers.

functions.name: django.views.decorators.http.require_http_methods
documentation:
Decorator to make a view only accept particular request methods.  Usage:

```
@require_http_methods(["GET", "POST"])
def my_view(request):
    # I can assume now that only GET or POST requests make it this far
    # ...
```

Note that request methods should be in uppercase.

functions.name: django.views.decorators.http.condition
documentation:
Decorator to support conditional retrieval (or change) for a view
function.

The parameters are callables to compute the ETag and last modified time for
the requested resource, respectively. The callables are passed the same
parameters as the view itself. The ETag function should return a string (or
None if the resource doesn't exist), while the last_modified function
should return a datetime object (or None if the resource doesn't exist).

The ETag function should return a complete ETag, including quotes (e.g.
'"etag"'), since that's the only way to distinguish between weak and strong
ETags. If an unquoted ETag is returned (e.g. 'etag'), it will be converted
to a strong ETag by adding quotes.

This decorator will either pass control to the wrapped view function or
return an HTTP 304 response (unmodified) or 412 response (precondition
failed), depending upon the request method. In either case, the decorator
will add the generated ETag and Last-Modified headers to the response if
the headers aren't already set and if the request's method is safe.

functions.name: django.views.decorators.http.etag
documentation: Shortcut decorators for common cases based on ETag or Last-Modified only

classes.name: django.utils.translation.Trans
documentation:
The purpose of this class is to store the actual translation function upon
receiving the first call to that function. After this is done, changes to
USE_I18N will have no effect to which function is served upon request. If
your tests rely on changing USE_I18N, you can delete all the functions
from _trans.__dict__.

Note that storing the function with setattr will have a noticeable
performance effect, as access to the function goes the normal path,
instead of using __getattr__.
Here be dragons, so a short explanation of the logic won't hurt:
We are trying to solve two problems: (1) access settings, in particular
settings.USE_I18N, as late as possible, so that modules can be imported
without having to first configure Django, and (2) if some other code creates
a reference to one of these functions, don't break that reference when we
replace the functions with their real counterparts (once we do access the
settings).

functions.name: django.views.static.serve
documentation:
Serve static files below a given point in the directory structure.

To use, put a URL pattern such as:

```
from django.views.static import serve

path('<path:path>', serve, {'document_root': '/path/to/my/files/'})
```

in your URLconf. You must provide the `document_root` param. You may
also set `show_indexes` to `True` if you'd like to serve a basic index
of the directory.  This index view will use the template hardcoded below,
but if you'd like to override it, you can create a template called
`static/directory_index.html`.

header
